elements.sent-2411-001: Williams\IEEEauthorrefmark{1 , Petar Popovski\IEEEauthorrefmark{1 \IEEEauthorblockA{\IEEEauthorrefmark{1 Department of Electronic Systems, Aalborg University, Denmark \IEEEauthorrefmark{2 Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of Waterloo, Canada email: mabouamer@uwaterloo.ca, rjw@es.aau.dk, petarp@es.aau.dk.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Digital twins (DTs) of wireless environments can be utilized to predict the propagation channel and reduce the overhead of required to estimate the channel statistics.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, direct channel prediction requires data-intensive calibration of the DT to capture the environment properties relevant for propagation of electromagnetic signals.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a framework that starts from a satellite image of the environment to produce an uncalibrated DT, which has no or imprecise information about the materials and their electromagnetic properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: The key idea is to use the uncalibrated DT to implicitly provide a geometric prior for the environment.
elements.sent-2411-006: This is utilized to inform a Gaussian process (GP), which permits the use of few channel measurements to attain an accurate prediction of the channel statistics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, the framework is able to quantify the uncertainty in channel statistics prediction and select rate in ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) that complies with statistical guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-008: The efficacy of the proposed geometry-informed GP is validated using experimental data obtained through a measurement campaign.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, the proposed prediction framework is shown to provide significant improvements compared to the benchmarks where i) direct channel statistics prediction is obtained using an uncalibrated DT and (ii) the GP predicts channel statistics using information about the location.
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, by maintaining a precise DT, site-specific digital twins can be used to reduce CSI acquisition and feedback overhead \cite{AK_DT_communication .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Additionally, DT-generated fingerprinting databases be used to improve positioning accuracy \cite{AK_DT_localization .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, for the DT to provide accurate predictions, its electromagnetic properties should match real-world channel conditions.
elements.sent-2411-013: The process of configuring the objects and materials in the DT to represent the wireless environment is referred to as calibration.
elements.sent-2411-014: Differentiable RTs allow the computation of gradients for system parameters, enabling tasks like data-driven material property optimization.
elements.sent-2411-015: This was demonstrated in recent works, such as \cite{RT_calibration,Sionna_RT_calibration , which leverage differentiable RT for material optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, to address phase errors caused by geometric discrepancies, \cite{Sionna_RT_calibration employs a variational expectation maximization algorithm for mitigating phase errors and thereby improving prediction accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The problem is to pick a data-rate which satisfies a set of reliability and latency constraints.
elements.sent-2411-018: Due to the latency constraint, channel feedback procedures are not always feasible.
elements.sent-2411-019: In such cases, the system must rely on alternatives such as channel statistics maps to select a rate which satisfies the reliability constraint with a sufficiently high probability.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the process of frequently calibrating a DT to achieve this level of accuracy is resource-intensive, restricting its practical use to limited scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-021: Rather than relying on a perfectly calibrated DT, the uncalibrated DT is employed to extract geometric features of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-022: These geometric features are combined with sparse channel measurements taken from a few anchor points to estimate the channel statistics across the entire scene.
elements.sent-2411-023: To enables this, we leverage the spatial interpolation capabilities of a Gaussian process (GP) along with the geometric consistency of the DT to predict channel statistics with high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-024: Thus, by providing a geometry-informed GP framework for channel prediction, this work aims to improve the overall reliability and performance of wireless systems in a more efficient and scalable manner.
elements.sent-2411-025: The results show{ that the proposed geometry-informed GP significantly improves prediction accuracy across the entire scene compared to the benchmarks where (i) an uncalibrated DT directly predicts channel statistics and (ii) a GP uses location information to predict channel statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, when the channel statistics map is used in rate selection for URLLC, the proposed framework is shown to (approximately) double the data rate while maintaining the same reliability, compared to the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-027: Notation: $\iu$ is the imaginary unit, $\abs{\cdot}$ is the absolute value, vectors are denoted by bold lowercase symbols, $\Aver{x}$ is the expectation operator.
elements.sent-2411-028: This work considers a single receiver and a single transmitter, both equipped with a single antenna each.
elements.sent-2411-029: The transmitter is a assumed to have access to an estimate of its location.
elements.sent-2411-030: The location of the receiver is static and known to all parties.
elements.sent-2411-031: Assuming an {Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) system, perfect clock synchronization, {a channel without Doppler shifts, and delay-spread equalization, such as cyclic-prefixing, the signal received at the receiver at a given subcarrier is: \begin{equation}y = \sqrt{P_tx} h\p{\vec{x}} s + n\end{equation} where $h\p{\vec{x \in \mathbb{C}$ is the frequency domain channel between the transmitter and receiver, $\vec{x}\in\mathbb{R ^{3\times 1}$ is the transmitter location, $s \in \mathbb{C}$ is a random transmit symbol with mean $\mu_s = \Aver{s = 0$ and variance $\sigma_s^2 = \Aver{s s\H = 1$, $P_\text{tx}\in \mathbb{R}$ is the time-averaged transmit power, and $n \sim \mathcal{CN}\p{0, \sigma_n^2}$ is additive white Gaussian noise.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The time-averaged signal to noise ratio is thus \begin{equation}\gamma\p{\vec{x}} = \Aver*{ \frac{\abs{\sqrt{P_tx} h \p{\vec{x}} s}^2}{\abs{n}^2} } = \frac{P_tx \abs{h\p{\vec{x}}}^2}{\sigma_n^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: The maximum rate that the channel can sustain under error-free communications is given through the channel capacity as \cite{yang_quasi-static_2014 \begin{equation}R_max = B \cdot \log_2\p{1 + \gamma\p{\vec{x}}}\end{equation} where $B$ is the bandwidth of the subcarrier.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Given known channel state information, the rate can thus be chosen in a straight forward manor as $R = R_\text{max}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The randomness is in part due to imperfect channel estimation, dynamic changes of the environment, and inaccurate position estimation.
elements.sent-2411-036: The DT can be used to remove some of the uncertainty from $h\p{\vec{x }$, which, in effect, changes the distribution of $h\p{\vec{x }$ from the perspective of a system designer.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The problem is then to find the maximum rate $R$ for which the probability of outage, $p_\text{out}$, is less than $\epsilon$; \begin{equation}\epsilon > p_out\p{R} = P\p{ R_max < R}, \notag = P\p{ B \cdot \log_2\p{1 + \frac{P_tx \abs{h\p{\vec{x}}}^2}{\sigma_n^2} } < R}, \notag \implies R < B \cdot \log_2\p{1 + \frac{P_tx p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x}}}{\sigma_n^2} }\end{equation} where $P\p{a < b}$ is the probability that $a < b$ and $p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$ is the $\epsilon$-quantile the channel power which ideally can be obtained from the of the cumulative probability distribution of $p\p{\vec{x = \abs{h\p{\vec{x ^2$, given that the distribution of $h\p{\vec{x }$ is known.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\epsilon > p_out\p{R} = P\p{ R_max < R}, \notag = P\p{ B \cdot \log_2\p{1 + \frac{P_tx \abs{h\p{\vec{x}}}^2}{\sigma_n^2} } < R}, \notag \implies R < B \cdot \log_2\p{1 + \frac{P_tx p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x}}}{\sigma_n^2} }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, due to latency constants, just as the instantaneous value of $h\p{\vec{x }$ is unknown, so is the distribution of $h\p{\vec{x }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Assuming, similarly to \cite{kallehauge2022predictive , that the the logarithm of the $\epsilon$-quantile channel power $q_\epsilon\p{\vec{x = \ln\p{p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$ behaves as a multivariate Gaussian random variable across space, the power quantiles can be predicted over space through Gaussian process regression, given a set of known power quantiles.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The estimation is done on the logarithm of the power quantiles as the logarithm provides a more Gaussian-like process.
elements.sent-2411-041: Consider the widely applied wide sense stationary uncorrelated scattering (WSSUS) channel model, in which the channel power, $p\p{\vec{x \sim \lambda\p{\vec{x \e{-\lambda\p{\vec{x p \,| \,p \geq 0$, follows an exponential distribution \cite{parsons_mobile_2000 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The natural logarithm of the channel power, $q\p{\vec{x = \ln\p{p\p{\vec{x \sim \lambda\p{\vec{x \e{q - \lambda\p{\vec{x \e{q }$, has a more Gaussian-like distribution and is thus better approximated by a multivariate Gaussian.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Obtaining spatial samples of the power quantiles, $p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$, necessary for ultra reliable communications, upon which to predict through Gaussian process regression, requires either relying on model based approaches which are susceptible to model mismatch errors, or a very high number of channel measurements to establish an empirical distribution, which results in high latency.}
elements.sent-2411-044: An alternative approach proposed in \cite{kallehauge2024experimental is to a range of subcarriers and use frequency as a proxy for space.}
elements.sent-2411-045: By measuring a wide bandwidth, an empirical channel power distribution can be established and an estimate of the $\epsilon$-quantile of the channel power, $p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$, can be extracted.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The validity of using frequency as a proxy for space can be argued by considering again the WSSUS Rayleigh fading channel \cite{parsons_mobile_2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the farfield of the transmitter, the impinging field can be written as a position dependent distribution of planewaves.
elements.sent-2411-048: In quantizing the distribution, the received signal in the vicinity of a point $\veci{x}$ can be written as \begin{equation}h\p{ \vec{x}, f} \approx s \sum_{n= 1}^N \alpha_n \e{-\iu k \vec{x}\T \vec{r}_n} \e{-\iu \tau_n f}\end{equation} where $k \in \mathbb{R}$ is the wavenumber, $ \vec{x \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times 1}$ is a point in the vicinity of $\veci{x}$ and $\alpha_n \in \mathbb{C}$ is the amplitude and phase, $\vec{r _n \in \left\{ \mathbb{R ^{3\times 1 | \vec{r _n\T \vec{r _n = 1 \right\}$ is the normal, and $\tau_n$ is the excess delay of the $n$'th planewave.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: In the Rayleigh channel, $\alpha_n$ is a set of zero mean independent identically distributed circular symmetric complex random variables.
elements.sent-2411-050: Independence between all $\tau_n$, $\vec{r _n$, and $\alpha_n$ is assumed.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Due to $\alpha_n$ being circular symmetric complex random variables, $\Aver{h\p{\vec{x , f = 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, due to the central limit theorem, $h\p{\vec{x , f \sim \mathcal{CN}\p{0, \sigma^2}$ for high $N$, where $\sigma^2 = \Aver*{\abs{h\p{\vec{x , f ^2}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: To allow relating the power quantile obtained from sampling across frequency to the power quantile obtained from sampling across space,.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{figures/scenario.png}\includegraphics[trim={10px 10px 10px 10px},clip,width=0.5\linewidth]{figures/sionnaRT.png} \caption{Two pictures of the scenario. Left: A satellite picture of the outdoor scenario not taken at the day of the measurement. Transmitter locations marked with blue dots \cite{kallehauge2024experimental}. Right: Illustration of the geometry made using the sionna.rt.scene.render python library \cite{sionna} and data extracted from OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps}. Transmitter points are marked as green dots.} \label{fig:scene_layout}
elements.sent-2411-054: The channel covariance function is given as \begin{equation}\Sigma^2\p{\vec{x}_1, \vec{x}_2, f_1, f_2} = \Aver*{h\p{\vec{x}_1, f_1} h^*\p{\vec{x}_2, f_2}}, \notag = \abs{s}^2 \sigma^2 c_\vec{x}\p{ \vec{x}_1 - \vec{x}_2} c_f\p{f_1 - f_2},\end{equation} where $c_\vec{x}\p{\vec{x = \Aver*{\e{-\iu k \vec{x \T \vec{r _n }$ is the spatial correlation function, and $c_f\p{f = \Aver*{ \e{-\iu \tau_n f }$ is the frequency correlation function.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\Sigma^2\p{\vec{x}_1, \vec{x}_2, f_1, f_2} = \Aver*{h\p{\vec{x}_1, f_1} h^*\p{\vec{x}_2, f_2}}, \notag = \abs{s}^2 \sigma^2 c_\vec{x}\p{ \vec{x}_1 - \vec{x}_2} c_f\p{f_1 - f_2},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: Assuming a uniform planewave normal distribution, $\vec{r _n = \begin{bmatrix \ec{\phi_n \es{\phi_n 0 \end{bmatrix}\T$ with $\phi_n \sim \mathcal{U}\p{0, 2\pi}$, as in Clarke's two-dimensional model \cite[Sec.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It can observed that samples are approximately uncorrelated, $c_\vec{x , c_f < 0.33$, for $x \geq 0.71 \lambda$ and $f \geq 4.6\, \tau_\text{max ^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In setting $\tau_\text{max = 10 d c^{-1}$ with $c$ being the speed of light in a vacuum and $d$ being the distance between transmitter and receiver, the channel is approximately uncorrelated for a frequency shift of $f = 1.4$ MHz at a distance of $100$ m, and approximately uncorrelated for a frequency shift of $f = 6.9$ MHz at a distance of $20$ m.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Thus, given sufficient bandwidth and distance, sampling across frequency and space is equivalent of drawing independent random samples from the same distribution.
elements.sent-2411-059: The usage of frequency as a proxy for space, is further verified through the DT in Sec.. \ref{sec:scenario .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Evaluation of the proposed system is done using the data from the measurement campaign described in \cite{kallehauge2024experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-061: The data consists of wideband channel measurements done with a stationary receiver and 127 different transmitter locations.
elements.sent-2411-062: Both the transmitter and receiver are equipped with omnidirectional antennas.
elements.sent-2411-063: The layout of the measurement campaign is illustrated on Fig. \ref{fig:scene_layout Left.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The layout of the digital twin of the same scenario is illustrated on Fig. \ref{fig:scene_layout Right, with the digital-twin (DT) equivalent 127 transmitter locations marked as green dots.}
elements.sent-2411-065: At each of the 127 locations, 8001 equispaced frequency domain channel measurements are made, spanning the bandwidth from 2 GHz to 10 GHz, yielding a frequency resolution of 1 MHz.
elements.sent-2411-066: The geometry used in the digital twin is extracted from OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps using the Blosm for Blender blender plugin and imported into the sionna python library \cite{sionna .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: All surfaces, ground included, are assigned the sionna-defined itu\_brick material which has a relative permittivity of $\epsilon_r = 3.91$ \cite[table 3]{ITU-R_P.2040-3 .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Approximating the statistics of small-scale fading via frequency-domain sampling
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figures/CDF_power_loc_freq_near_55_more_info_new_font.pdf} \subcaption{ Near the transmitter } \label{fig:CDF near } \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width= 0.95\linewidth]{figures/CDF_power_loc_freq_far_111_more_info_new_font.pdf} \subcaption{ Far from the transmitter} \label{fig:CDF far} \end{minipage} \caption{{Frequency based and location based CDF of received power (left) near transmitter and (right) far from the transmitter.}} \label{fig:freq_loc_based}
elements.sent-2411-068: We verify this claim using the geometry-consistent DT of the scene.
elements.sent-2411-069: Using the channel impulse response (CIR) generated by the DT, an empirical location-based distribution of the channel power in dB is computed.
elements.sent-2411-070: The empirical frequency-based channel power distribution is obtained by fixing the transmitter's position and sampling the frequency domain over a large bandwidth (i.e., 8 GHz, as suggested by \cite{kallehauge2024experimental ).}
elements.sent-2411-071: The results are shown for two typical transmitter positions (a) near the transmitter (position 55 in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout ) and (b) far from the transmitter (position 111 in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout ).}}
elements.sent-2411-072: As Fig.~\ref{fig:freq_loc_based shows, the frequency-domain channel power distribution closely approximates the location-based channel power distribution, including the tails of the distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For example, when the receiver is far from the transmitter, the 0.01-quantiles of the location-based and frequency-based distributions differ by only 0.02 dB.
elements.sent-2411-074: Thus, frequency-domain samples, which are can be obtained by wideband channel sounding, offers a practical method to approximate small-scale fading distributions that are otherwise challenging to capture directly \cite{kallehauge2024experimental .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/ground_truth.pdf} \subcaption{ Ground truth} \label{fig:Ground truth} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width= \linewidth, ]{figures/predicted_map_proposed.pdf} \subcaption{Prediction by proposed GP} \label{fig:Prediction by (Uncalibrated) DT} \end{minipage} \caption{{Closest point interpolation of $\epsilon$-quantile of fading in dB associated with (ground truth) measurements and predictions by proposed GP and $D=30$ observations. }} \label{fig:estimated quantiles}
elements.sent-2411-075: Due to small scale fading, $p(\vec{x )$ is modeled as a random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-076: As demonstrated in \cite{kallehauge2024experimental , reliable rate selection can be achieved using accurate estimates of the statistics of $p(\vec{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Particularly, The goal is to estimate the log $\epsilon$-quantile $q_{\epsilon (\vec{x )$ \begin{equation}\label{quantile def} \rho_{\epsilon}(\vec{x}) = \sup\{\rho > 0 | P(p(\vec{x}) \leq \rho) < \epsilon)\} \nonumber q_{\epsilon}(\vec{x}) = \ln (\rho_{\epsilon}(\vec{x}))\end{equation} {For each position $\vec{x}$, a DT can ideally be used to estimate the CDF of the received power ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x ) = P(p(\vec{x ) \leq \beta)$ which can then be directly used to provide an estimate $q^{\sf{direct _{\epsilon (\vec{x )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, in this work, we consider uncalibrated DTs generated from open-source maps (e.g. OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps ) and with default material properties.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Thus, due to mismatched material properties, changes in the wireless environment and other non-idealities, the direct use of a DT does not necessarily provide an accurate estimate of $q_{\epsilon (\vec{x )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: However, the DT's received power CDF ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x )$ can be leveraged to provide site-specific geometric insights.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We propose to exploit the geometric information implicitly conveyed by ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (.)$ and a small number of channel measurements to predict fading statistics across the entire site.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The quantile function $q_{\epsilon (\vec{x )$ is modeled as GP.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: As demonstrated in \cite{kallehauge2024experimental , GP regression can be used to predict fading statistics by spatially interpolating channel measurements using a predictive distribution \begin{equation}q_{\epsilon}^{\sf{spatial}} (\vec{x}| D) \sim N (\mu(\vec{x}| D), \sigma^{2} (\vec{x}| D)).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this work, instead of relying on spatial features alone, we employ GP regression to predict fading statistics using geometric features derived from the DT.
elements.sent-2411-085: In particular, by processing the DT's distribution of received power $\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta,\vec{x )$, we employ a predictive distribution \begin{equation}\label{GP proposed} q^{\sf{DT}}_{\epsilon} (\vec{x}| D) \sim N (\mu_{\sf{DT}}(\vec{y}| D), \sigma_{\sf{DT}}^2( \vec{y}| D)), \vec{y} = (\vec{x}, \vec{f} (\vec{\psi}^{\sf{DT}}(\beta,\vec{x}))).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{GP proposed} q^{\sf{DT}}_{\epsilon} (\vec{x}| D) \sim N (\mu_{\sf{DT}}(\vec{y}| D), \sigma_{\sf{DT}}^2( \vec{y}| D)), \vec{y} = (\vec{x}, \vec{f} (\vec{\psi}^{\sf{DT}}(\beta,\vec{x}))).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: With this $\vec{y}$ and the use of an anisotropic GP kernel $k(\vec{y , \vec{y ')$, the GP does not only capture spatial similarities but also implicitly encodes geometric information provided by the DT.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Particularly, for two receiver positions $\vec{x _1$ and $\vec{x _2$ that result in similar DT outputs $\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x _1) \approx \vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x _2)$, the GP's covariance function ensures that the predicted channel statistics at these two locations are close to each other.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Thus, the DT's geometric features are captured via the GP's covariance function, embedding geometric correlations that allows efficient prediction of channel statistics using a limited number of measurements.
elements.sent-2411-089: Moreover, this proposed prediction framework is shown to provide significant improvements in prediction errors compared to the following benchmarks:.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.77\linewidth]{figures/errors_map_RT.pdf} \subcaption{Prediction errors by (uncalibrated) DT} \label{fig:Prediction errors RT} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.77\linewidth,]{figures/errors_map_baseline.pdf} \subcaption{ Prediction errors by baseline GP} \label{fig:Prediction errors baseline GP} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\linewidth} \captionsetup{justification=centering,margin=1cm} \includegraphics[width=0.86\linewidth]{figures/errors_map_proposed.pdf} \subcaption{Prediction errors by proposed GP with DT features} \label{fig:prediction errors proposed GP} \end{minipage} \caption{{Absolute Error in predicting $\epsilon$-quantile of fading using DT, GP baseline and Proposed GP.}} \label{fig:GP_baseline_proposed}

elements.sent-2411-096: As shown in the figure, compared to the benchmarks, the proposed method significantly improves prediction accuracy across the entire scene by effectively leveraging the geometric consistency of the DT.
elements.sent-2411-097: For instance, the proposed prediction scheme reduces the median error to $1.8$ dB compared to $3.7$ dB and $3.2$ dB associated with $q^{\sf{direct _{\epsilon (\vec{x )$ and $q_{\epsilon ^{\sf{spatial (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-099: Following \cite{kallehauge2024experimental , $R^{\sf{DT (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$ is selected using the predictive distribution $q^{\sf{DT _{\epsilon (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$ given in \eqref{GP proposed .}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-101: In particular, for predictive mean $\mu_{\sf{DT (\vec{y | \mathcal{D )$ and predictive variance $\sigma_{\sf{DT ^2( \vec{y | \mathcal{D )$, \begin{equation}R^{\sf{DT}} (\vec{x}| D) = \log_2 \Big(1 + \e { \mu_{\sf{DT}}(\vec{y}| D) + \sqrt{2} \sigma_{\sf{DT}}( \vec{y}| D) \erf^{-1} (2 \delta-1) } \Big). \notag\end{equation} The predictive distribution $q_{\epsilon ^{\sf{spatial (\vec{x | \mathcal{D ) \sim \mathcal{N (\mu(\vec{x | \mathcal{D ), \sigma^{2 (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$ can be similarly used to select a corresponding rate select $R^{\sf{spatial (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-106: For instance, across different locations, the average normalized rate is approximately doubled from $\bar{\hat{R ^{\sf{spatial _{\epsilon (\vec{x )=0.27$ from to $\bar{\hat{R ^{\sf{DT _{\epsilon (\vec{x )= 0.45$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, compared to $q^{\sf{spatial _{\epsilon (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$, $q^{\sf{DT _{\epsilon (\vec{x | \mathcal{D )$ allows selection of higher rates while complying with reliability constraints.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/CDF_of_Normalized_Rates_fixed.pdf} \caption{{Empirical distribution of $R^{\sf{DT}} (\vec{x}| \mathcal{D})$ and $R^{\sf{spatial}} (\vec{x}| \mathcal{D})$ normalized by $\epsilon$-outage capacity $R_{\epsilon}(\vec{x})$ for $\epsilon=1 \%$ and $\delta= 10 \%$.} } \label{fig:estimated rates ratio}
elements.sent-2411-108: In this paper we have shown that by incorporating raytraced channel information from an inaccurate DT, one can improve the accuracy of channel statistics prediction.
elements.sent-2411-109: The proposed system is capable of almost doubling the provided data rate while maintaining the same reliability, when compared with the baseline.
elements.sent-2411-110: This increase does not come at the expense of increased overhead and lengthy DT calibration procedures, as the system utilizes an uncalibrated DT based on geometry from OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps with all objects, ground included, having material parameters equal to that of a brick wall.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The proposed framework successfully utilizes a DT based on freely available geometry data without calibration to directly improve the performance of a communication system.. \bibliographystyle{setup/IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-112: We introduce a framework for predicting wireless channel statistics based on digital twin (DT) and ray tracing.
elements.sent-2411-113: The DT is derived from satellite images and is uncalibrated, as it does not assume precise information on the electromagnetic properties of the materials in the environment.
elements.sent-2411-114: The uncalibrated DT is utilized to derive a geometric prior that informs a Gaussian process (GP) and thereby predict channel statistics using only a few measurements.
elements.sent-2411-115: The framework also quantifies uncertainty, offering statistical guarantees for rate selection in ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC).
elements.sent-2411-116: Experimental validation demonstrates the efficacy of the proposed framework using measurement data.
elements.sent-2411-117: Digital twins (DTs) that accurately model wireless channels have demonstrated significant utility for both communication and localization tasks \cite{survey_DT .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Differentiable ray tracers (RT), such as Sionna RT \cite{Sionna_RT are instrumental in enabling effective calibration methods.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Differentiable RTs allow the computation of gradients for system parameters, enabling tasks like data-driven material property optimization \cite{RT_calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-120: This work addresses resource allocation for ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) communications, aiming to select a data rate that meets a statistical reliability constraint.
elements.sent-2411-121: Due to latency limitations, channel feedback may be infeasible and hence the system must rely on alternatives such as channel statistics maps to select a rate which satisfies the reliability constraint with a sufficiently high probability.
elements.sent-2411-122: While a fully calibrated DT can directly predict channel statistics by accurately modeling objects and materials, frequent calibration is resource-intensive and limits its practical use.
elements.sent-2411-123: To address this, we focus on exploiting uncalibrated DTs that can be directly derived from satellite images available through open-source maps like OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps , without relying on prior knowledge of material properties in the scene or requiring data-intensive calibration processes.}
elements.sent-2411-124: We summarize our contributions and results as follows.
elements.sent-2411-125: An uncalibrated DT is used to extract geometric features of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-126: These are combined with channel measurements from a few anchor points to estimate the channel statistics across the entire scene.
elements.sent-2411-127: The estimation leverages the spatial interpolation capabilities of a Gaussian process (GP) along with the geometric consistency of the DT to predict channel statistics with high accuracy in the region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-128: This is validated via experimental data obtained through a measurement campaign.
elements.sent-2411-129: The results show that the proposed geometry-informed GP significantly improves prediction accuracy across the entire scene compared to the benchmarks where (i) an uncalibrated DT directly predicts channel statistics; and (ii) a GP uses location information to predict channel statistics.
elements.sent-2411-130: Moreover, when the channel statistics map is used in rate selection for URLLC, the proposed framework is shown to, approximately, double the data rate while maintaining the same reliability, compared to the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-131: In summary, the geometry-informed GP framework scales the small number of calibration measurements to channel predictions that are statistically accurate for the entire region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-132: Section~\ref{sec:system_model presents the problem formulation, and the construction of the uncalibrated DT.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Section~\ref{sec: Site-specific Statistics Prediction presents the channel-prediction framework, and Section~\ref{sec:scenario presents an effective method for experimentally measuring channel-fading statistics.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Finally, Section~\ref{sec:simulation details the rate-selection framework and its experimental validation.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We consider a system where an access point (AP) serves a user (UE) located at position $\vec{x}$.
elements.sent-2411-136: The UE, equipped with a single antenna, acts as the transmitter (TX) and transmits its zero-mean, unit-power symbol $s \in \mathbb{C}$ with transmit power $P_\text{tx \geq 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: The single-antenna AP then functions as the receiver (RX).
elements.sent-2411-138: The received narrowband signal, after delay-spread equalization, is \begin{equation}y = \sqrt{P_tx} h\p{\vec{x}} s + n,\end{equation} where $h\p{\vec{x \in \mathbb{C}$ is the channel between the transmitter and receiver, and $n \sim \mathcal{CN}\p{0, \sigma_n^2}$ is additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN).}}
elements.sent-2411-140: If the distribution of $h\p{\vec{x }$ is known, then $R(\vec{x )$ can be selected using the statistics of the channel power $p(\vec{x )= |h\p{\vec{x |^2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: However, due to latency constraints and the dynamic properties of the wireless communication channel, the distribution of $p(\vec{x )$ is not perfectly known.}
elements.sent-2411-142: To account for the uncertainty associated with $p_{\text{out , \vec{x }$}, this work aims to maximize the rate $R (\vec{x )$ that can be selected while ensuring compliance with a reliability constraint that bounds the meta-probability as $\tilde{p _{\epsilon \overset{\Delta {= P\p{ p_\text{out,$\vec{x}$} > \epsilon \leq \delta$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: This can also be expressed as \cite{kallehauge2024experimental \begin{equation}\label{meta_probability} \tilde{p} _{\epsilon}= P\p{ R(\vec{x}) > \log_2\p{1 + \frac{P_tx p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x}}}{\sigma_n^2} } } \leq \delta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Here, $p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$ is the $\epsilon$-quantile the channel power.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In this work, we rely on statistical radio maps to estimate the power quantiles $p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$.}
elements.sent-2411-146: To this end, we employ an uncalibrated digital twin (DT) as a geometric prior to inform a Gaussian process (GP), enabling channel-statistic predictions from only a few measurements.
elements.sent-2411-147: Traditional radio maps require dense measurements and lack geometric context.
elements.sent-2411-148: On the other hand, while capturing the underlying geometry, uncalibrated digital twins yield inaccurate channel statistics, as ray tracing relies on assumed electromagnetic properties that may not reflect real materials.
elements.sent-2411-149: This mismatch distorts the received power, impacting the prediction of URLLC-relevant statistics (i.e., lower quantiles).
elements.sent-2411-150: To address these limitations, our proposed framework, presented in Section~\ref{sec: Site-specific Statistics Prediction , leverages geometry-based DT outputs to embed spatial correlations within a GP, which utilizes a small set of measurements to accurately predict the channel statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-151: GPs are employed here for their sample-efficient spatial interpolation properties, enabling not only efficient prediction but also closed-form uncertainty quantification \cite{Gp_book .}
elements.sent-2411-152: Subsequently, in contrast to conventional radio maps that provide point estimates of average statistics, the proposed framework provides predictions of URLLC-relevant statistics along with closed-form quantification of prediction uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-153: This enables the selection of transmission rates under statistical guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-154: In Section~\ref{sec:simulation , we validate our GP-based framework using real measurements, demonstrating its ability to accurately predict channel statistics and meet URLLC reliability targets.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, height= 5.5cm]{abouamer_WCL2025-1091_fig1.eps} \caption{Bottom right: A satellite picture of the outdoor scenario not taken at the day of the measurement. Transmitter locations marked with blue dots \cite{kallehauge2024experimental}. Top left: Illustration of the geometry made using the sionna.rt.scene.render python library \cite{Sionna_RT} and data extracted from OpenStreetMap \cite{OpenStreetmaps}. Transmitter locations are marked as green dots.} \label{fig:scene_layout}
elements.sent-2411-155: We consider the scenario illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout (Left), featuring a stationary receiver and 127 distinct transmitter locations, each equipped with an omnidirectional antenna.}
elements.sent-2411-156: A digital twin (DT) of this environment was created by extracting the geometry from OpenStreetMap\cite{OpenStreetmaps using the Blosm for Blender plugin, and importing it into the Sionna Python library\cite{Sionna_RT .}}
elements.sent-2411-157: In the DT (Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout (Right)), the 127 transmitter sites are marked as green dots.}
elements.sent-2411-158: All surfaces, including the ground, are modeled with Sionna’s predefined itu\_brick material (relative permittivity $\epsilon_r = 3.91$) \cite[Table~3]{ITU-R_P.2040-3 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: No calibration of the DT is performed.
elements.sent-2411-160: In this section, we focus on channel statistics prediction, with the rate-selection problem addressed and validated using experimental data in Section~\ref{section: Rate selection under reliability constraints .}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Data Preprocessing and Estimation of Quantiles
elements.sent-2411-161: Consider an input dataset $\mathcal{\hat{D =\{\vec{p _d,\vec{x _d\}_{d=1 ^D$, where for each location $\vec{x _d$, $\vec{p _d= \left( p_{d,1 , \ \ldots ,\ p_{d,N \right)$ captures $N$ independent small-scale fading channel measurements.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: To enable spatial channel prediction, the logarithm of the $\epsilon$-quantile channel power, defined as $q_\epsilon\p{\vec{x = \ln\p{p_\epsilon\p{\vec{x }$, is modeled as a Gaussian process (GP).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: The logarithmic transformation is applied to allow transformed data to be effectively modeled as a GP.
elements.sent-2411-164: To estimate the log $\epsilon$-quantile $q_{\epsilon (\vec{x )$, the log-fading power quantile at a location $ \vec{x _d$ is estimated as $\widehat{q _{\epsilon,d = \ln\left({p _{d,(r)}\right)$ for $r = \lfloor N\epsilon \rfloor$ and $p_{d,(r)}$ denoting the $r$-th order statistic of $\vec{p _d$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: Subsequently, the input to the channel prediction scheme is a dataset $\mathcal{{D =\{\widehat{q _{\epsilon,d ,\vec{x _d\}_{d=1 ^D$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: By modeling the quantile function $q_\epsilon\p{\vec{x $ as a Gaussian process, GP regression can be used to predict fading statistics by interpolating channel measurements resulting in a predictive distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-167: For the implementation details, we refer the reader to \cite[Sec.. III-B]{kallehauge2022predictive .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Notably, in this work, we go beyond spatial interpolation by incorporating geometric correlations extracted from an uncalibrated DT to enable geometry-inspired statistical predictions.
elements.sent-2411-169: For each position $\vec{x}$, a DT can be used to obtain the CDF of the received power ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x ) = P(p(\vec{x ) \leq \beta)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: If the DT is perfectly calibrated then ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x )$ can be directly used to provide an estimate $q^{\sf{direct _{\epsilon (\vec{x )$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: However, the DT's received power CDF ${\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x )$ can be leveraged to provide site-specific geometric features.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: In particular, by processing the DT's distribution of received power $\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta,\vec{x )$, we employ the following predictive distribution \begin{equation}\label{GP proposed} q^{\sf{DT}}_{\epsilon} (\vec{x}| D) \sim N (\mu_{\sf{DT}}(\vec{y}| D), \sigma_{\sf{DT}}^2( \vec{y}| D)), \nonumber \vec{y} = (\vec{x}, \vec{f} (\vec{\psi}^{\sf{DT}}(\beta,\vec{x}))).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{GP proposed} q^{\sf{DT}}_{\epsilon} (\vec{x}| D) \sim N (\mu_{\sf{DT}}(\vec{y}| D), \sigma_{\sf{DT}}^2( \vec{y}| D)), \nonumber \vec{y} = (\vec{x}, \vec{f} (\vec{\psi}^{\sf{DT}}(\beta,\vec{x}))).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-173: With the $\vec{y}$ defined above, the GP kernel $k(\vec{y , \vec{y ')$ not only captures spatial patterns but also implicitly encodes geometric information provided by the DT.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: In particular, for two receiver positions $\vec{x _1$ and $\vec{x _2$ that result in similar DT outputs, $\vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x _1) \approx \vec{\psi ^{\sf{DT (\beta, \vec{x _2)$, the GP's covariance function ensures that the predicted channel statistics at these two locations are similar.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: Before presenting the experimental results, which corroborate the efficacy of our framework, the next section provides theoretical and experimental justification for an effective method for the experimental characterization of small-scale fading statistics.
elements.section-2411-008: Experimental Characterization of Small-Scale Fading Statistics
elements.sent-2411-176: An alternative approach is to use a range of subcarriers and use frequency as a proxy for space \cite{kallehauge2024experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-177: In quantizing the distribution, the received signal in the vicinity of a point $\veci{x}$ can be written as $h\p{ \vec{x , f \approx \sum_{n= 1 ^N \alpha_n \e{-\iu k \vec{x}\T \vec{r _n \e{-\iu \tau_n f}$, where $k \in \mathbb{R}$ is the wavenumber, $ \vec{x \in \mathbb{R ^{3\times 1}$ is a point in the vicinity of $\veci{x}$ and $\alpha_n \in \mathbb{C}$ is the amplitude and phase, $\vec{r _n \in \left\{ \mathbb{R ^{3\times 1 | \vec{r _n\T \vec{r _n = 1 \right\}$ is the normal vector indicating the direction of propagation, and $\tau_n$ is the excess delay of the $n$'th planewave.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: To allow relating the power quantile obtained from sampling across frequency to the power quantile obtained from sampling across space, the covariance properties of the channel across frequency and space, is analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-179: The channel covariance function is given by $\Sigma^2\p{\vec{x _1, \vec{x _2, f_1, f_2 = \Aver*{h\p{\vec{x _1, f_1 h^*\p{\vec{x _2, f_2 , = \abs{s ^2 \sigma^2 c_\vec{x}\p{\vec{x _1 - \vec{x _2 c_f\p{f_1 - f_2 ,$ where $c_\vec{x}\p{\vec{x = \Aver*{\e{-\iu k \vec{x \T \vec{r _n }$ is the spatial correlation function, and $c_f\p{f = \Aver*{ \e{-\iu \tau_n f }$ is the frequency correlation function.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width= \linewidth, height=4.5cm]{abouamer_WCL2025-1091_fig2.eps} \caption{{Frequency-based and location-based CDF of received power for two TX positions, one near the receiver (position 55 in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout}) and another far from the receiver (position 111).}} \label{fig:freq_loc_based}
elements.sent-2411-180: For each of the 127 transmitter locations shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout , the small-scale fading distribution is computed by employing the DT to generate a grid of 400 small movements (on the order of a wavelength $\lambda$) in the transmitter's $x$ and $y$ coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The empirical frequency-based channel power distribution is also obtained using the DT by fixing the transmitter's position and sampling the frequency domain over a large bandwidth (i.e., 8 GHz, as suggested by \cite{kallehauge2024experimental ).}
elements.sent-2411-182: Fig.~\ref{fig:freq_loc_based shows the empirical cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of received power for both the frequency-based and location-based approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The results are shown for two typical transmitter positions, one near the receiver and another far from the receiver.
elements.sent-2411-184: Thus, frequency-domain samples, obtained by wideband channel sounding, offer a practical method to approximate small-scale fading distributions that are otherwise challenging to capture directly.
elements.sent-2411-185: At each of the 127 transmitter locations shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:scene_layout , wideband channel measurements were performed in the frequency domain.}
elements.sent-2411-186: A total of 8\,001 equally spaced frequency samples were taken per site, spanning the band from $2$GHz to $10$GHz, resulting in a frequency resolution of $1$MHz.
elements.sent-2411-187: During each measurement, the receiver remained stationary while the transmitter was sequentially positioned at the designated locations.
elements.sent-2411-188: Detailed information on the hardware, and measurement protocols can be found in \cite{kallehauge2024experimental .}

elements.sent-2411-207: In this paper we have shown that by incorporating ray-traced channel information from an inaccurate DT, one can improve the accuracy of channel statistics prediction.
elements.sent-2411-208: The proposed framework successfully utilizes a DT based on freely available geometry data without calibration to directly improve the performance of a communication system.. \appendices \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
